narrative: You spot a large flat rosette of enormous heart-shaped leaves at the edge of the path. The underside of the leaves is covered in a soft white wool...
interesting_fact: The hooked burrs of Burdock directly inspired Swiss engineer George de Mestral to invent Velcro in the 1940s after finding them stuck to his dog's fur.

questions.question: Which of these plants is Greater Burdock?
Does Burdock have any other common names?
options: Beggar's Buttons and Gobo
Goosegrass and Sticky Willy
Alehoof and Creeping Charlie
Winterweed and Starwort
questions.remember: Gobo is the Japanese name — burdock root is a prized vegetable in Japanese cuisine and widely cultivated there. Beggar's Buttons refers to the burrs that cling to clothing like buttons

questions.question: Where is Burdock typically found?
options: Roadsides, pathways, field edges and wasteland on nutrient-rich disturbed soil
Damp woodland edges and hedgerows on heavy clay soil
Meadows and grassland on well-drained chalky soils
Field margins and arable land on fertile loamy soil
questions.correct: Roadsides, pathways, field edges and wasteland on nutrient-rich disturbed soil

questions.question: What are the botanical names for Greater and Lesser Burdock?
options: Arctium lappa / Arctium minus
Arctium tomentosum / Arctium nanum
Arctium vulgare / Arctium sylvestre
Arctium pubens / Arctium campestre
questions.remember: Both species are edible and interchangeable — Greater Burdock has solid leaf stalks, Lesser Burdock has hollow ones

questions.question: Select all key identifying features of Burdock
options: Very large heart-shaped leaves with a woolly white underside
Round burrs with hooked spines that cling to clothing and fur
Purple thistle-like flowers in summer
Finely toothed leaf edges with a strong bitter smell when crushed
Hollow ridged stems with a distinctly waxy green surface
correct: Very large heart-shaped leaves with a woolly white underside
Round burrs with hooked spines that cling to clothing and fur

questions.question: What is the life cycle of Burdock?
options: Biennial — rosette of leaves in year one, flowers and dies in year two
Perennial — returns every year from the same rootstock
Annual — completes its full life cycle within a single growing season
Ephemeral — appears briefly in spring then dies back completely within weeks
questions.correct: Biennial — rosette of leaves in year one, flowers and dies in year two
questions.remember: In year one the plant is a low flat rosette with no flowering stem — in year two it shoots up to 2m tall with branching stems and burrs. Harvest the root in year one autumn before the plant bolts

questions.question: What is Burdock used for? Select all that apply
options: Root eaten raw when young or cooked in stir fries and soups
Peeled stems blanched and eaten as a vegetable
Large leaves used to wrap food parcels for cooking over a fire
Seeds dried and ground into a spice for seasoning meat
correct: Root eaten raw when young or cooked in stir fries and soups
Peeled stems blanched and eaten as a vegetable
Large leaves used to wrap food parcels for cooking over a fire

questions.question: Is there anything to be aware of legally when foraging Burdock root?
options: Yes — foraging roots requires the landowner's permission as it counts as uprooting a plant
No — all parts of Burdock can be freely foraged anywhere in the UK
Only in designated nature reserves — it is freely available elsewhere
Yes — it is listed under the Weeds Act 1959 and cannot be removed without a licence
questions.correct: Yes — foraging roots requires the landowner's permission as it counts as uprooting a plant

questions.question: When is the best time to harvest Burdock root?
options: Autumn of the first year — before it becomes woody and the plant puts energy into flowering
Spring of the second year — after winter frosts have softened the root and improved the flavour
Midsummer of the first year — when the root is at its longest and most nutritious
Autumn of the second year — after the plant has finished flowering and set seed
questions.correct: Autumn of the first year — before it becomes woody and the plant puts energy into flowering

questions.question: What does Burdock root taste like?
options: Sweet and mildly earthy — similar to parsnip crossed with artichoke
Bitter and strongly astringent
Sharp and lemony
Mild and starchy like potato
questions.correct: Sweet and mildly earthy — similar to parsnip crossed with artichoke

questions.question: What are the cautions with Burdock?
options: May cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals — avoid during pregnancy due to oestrogenic effects. Contains inulin which can cause wind
Contains oxalic acid — avoid in large quantities especially if prone to kidney stones
Safe with no known cautions — can be eaten freely in any quantity
Avoid entirely as the root contains compounds that can cause liver damage in large quantities
questions.correct: May cause contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals — avoid during pregnancy due to oestrogenic effects. Contains inulin which can cause wind

questions.question: Why should you avoid foraging Burdock root from roadsides or industrial areas?
options: The roots can absorb heavy metals and toxins from the soil
The roots become bitter and unpalatable when grown in compacted urban soil
The burrs from urban plants contain higher levels of irritating compounds
Roadside plants are legally protected and cannot be foraged
questions.correct: The roots can absorb heavy metals and toxins from the soil

questions.question: Which toxic plant could Burdock be confused with?
options: Foxglove (Digitalis purpurea) — similar large leaves but more pointed with no woolly underside
Hemlock (Conium maculatum) — similar tall stems in disturbed habitats
Lords and Ladies (Arum maculatum) — similar large leaves in shaded habitats
Ragwort (Senecio jacobaea) — similar height and disturbed ground preference
questions.correct: Foxglove (Digitalis purpurea) — similar large leaves but more pointed with no woolly underside

questions.question: When does Burdock flower?
options: July to September
March to May
October to December
April to June

questions.question: Is Burdock native to the UK?
options: Yes — native to Europe and widely naturalised across Britain and Ireland
No — introduced from Japan where it is cultivated as a vegetable crop
No — introduced from North America in the 18th century as a medicinal herb
Yes — but only in southern England, rare north of the Midlands
questions.correct: Yes — native to Europe and widely naturalised across Britain and Ireland

questions.question: What was Burdock historically used to treat? Select all that apply
options: Skin conditions such as eczema, psoriasis and boils
Liver complaints and as a blood purifier
Urinary tract infections
Broken bones and fractures
correct: Skin conditions such as eczema, psoriasis and boils
Liver complaints and as a blood purifier

questions.question: How was Burdock applied medicinally? Select all that apply
options: Root eaten or made into a tea or decoction
Leaves applied as a poultice to skin conditions
Burned and inhaled as smoke
Seeds ground into a powder and swallowed dry
correct: Root eaten or made into a tea or decoction
Leaves applied as a poultice to skin conditions

questions.question: What plant family does Burdock belong to?
options: Asteraceae — the daisy family
Lamiaceae — the mint family
Rosaceae — the rose family
Polygonaceae — the dock and sorrel family

questions.question: What is the possible origin of the name Arctium?
options: Possibly from Greek arktos meaning bear — possibly referring to the rough shaggy appearance of the burrs
Possibly from Greek arktikos meaning northern — possibly referring to its preference for cool climates
Possibly from Latin arcus meaning curved — possibly referring to the hooked spines on the burrs
Possibly from Greek arche meaning ancient — possibly referring to its long history of medicinal use
questions.correct: Possibly from Greek arktos meaning bear — possibly referring to the rough shaggy appearance of the burrs

questions.question: What does Lappa mean in the botanical name?
options: From Latin lappare meaning to seize — referring to the clinging ability of the burrs
From Greek lapas meaning flat — referring to the broad shape of the leaves
From Latin lapsus meaning fallen — referring to how the burrs fall and disperse seeds
From Celtic lapp meaning large — referring to the impressive size of the plant
questions.correct: From Latin lappare meaning to seize — referring to the clinging ability of the burrs

questions.question: What everyday invention was directly inspired by Burdock?
options: Velcro — Swiss engineer George de Mestral invented it in the 1940s after finding burrs stuck to his dog's fur
The safety pin — inspired by the hooked spines locking together on the burrs
Zip fasteners — inspired by the interlocking hooks on the dried seed heads
Sticky tape — inspired by the adhesive surface of the burr when pressed flat
questions.correct: Velcro — Swiss engineer George de Mestral invented it in the 1940s after finding burrs stuck to his dog's fur
